published_date: 2026-05-26

url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Bzlmba-BMW0
title: The Macro Minute: Should the Fed look through the latest inflationary supply shock?

Happy Tuesday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, with our
macro minute for Tuesday, May 26th,
2026. Hope everyone had an enjoyable
Memorial Day holiday with their
families. We just want to say we thank
all the families of our fallen soldiers
out there. We are deeply, deeply
grateful for your sacrifice. And we just
want to uplift you today with that
message. So, as always, we'll start with
the executive summary from today's
lead-off morning note. Today's key macro
question is, should the Fed look through
the latest inflationary supply shock?
Our answer is no. Inflation will likely
remain the market's primary focus this
week with Thursday's April PC report
serving as a key input for the June 16th
to 17th FOMC meeting, which will be the
first under Fed Chair Kevin Warsh. In
our view, market participants are
generally not doing enough work to
determine how nominally hot the US
economy is separate and apart from the
energy price shock. Because of this lazy
broad acceptance of the consensus
narrative that inflation will be under
control if not for the US-Israel Iran
conflict, we continue to observe many
investors calling for the Fed to look
through these price pressures.
The risk of the Fed committing a second
inflation accident and contributing to a
second stock market bubble in a half
decade is high amid pervasive group
think.
As always, we'll wrap up with a question
from our community.
In the 42 macro dashboard, this one's
titled vacation season. Not quite a
question, but certainly a comment worthy
of unpacking. It says, "So, is anyone
else traveling this weekend? The high
gas and airline prices seem not to be
affecting people to travel. I traveled
on Tuesday last week and both legs of my
flight were 100% full. I've been in
Coeur d'Alene since Tuesday. My hotel's
been sold out. People everywhere and
young families are here. It was a
surprise to me." So, the thing I The
reason I wanted to highlight this is
because this is exactly what we've been
talking about for years in the context
of our West Village Montauk effect and
all the K-shaped policy dynamics that
are contributing to our K-shaped
economy. What you are witnessing here,
uh talking to the to the member here,
what you're witnessing is a planes full
of rich people and hotels full of rich
people who who have plenty of money,
whose incomes are being supported by
obviously a you know, a rapidly
ascending equity market and an equity
market that has compounded at about plus
20% in each of the past 3 years and it's
well on its way to doing that again here
in 2026. And so that's those are
contributing factors as well. Don't
forget we have a tremendous amount of
K-shaped fiscal policy as well. When you
look at the amount of money that the
federal government spends on on
means-tested programs relative to
everything else, everything else being
national defense, net interest on the
debt and everything else the government
spends on, the government spends
basically twice as much money on
everything else as it does on poor
people. And so when you think about the
you know, the multi-million dollar
contracts that are coming out from
national defense to this company or that
company, you think about the trillion
plus dollars of net interest that the
government spends on on on you know,
supporting the US
sovereign debt market,
you know, that money winds up in the
hands of rich people. It doesn't wind up
in the hands of poor people. Again, only
about 1.3 trillion dollars of our
annualized outlays here in 2026 wind up
in the hands of poor people, whereas
about 2.6 trillion dollars winds up in
the hands of rich people, folks like
Elon Musk, Alex Karp and all the folks
that work at companies like that that
sell services to the government. So
that's another dynamic and in the final
dynamic I highlight is the the West
Village Montauk effect itself. I recall
that the West Village Montauk effect is
an effect that is an economic dynamic
that we authored a few years ago in the
context of our resilient US economy
theme that essentially says because we
have such a high stock of savings on
household sector balance sheets, the
flow of savings can actually decline and
has been structurally and cyclically and
structurally depressed for years now in
a way that has been supportive of
aggregate consumption. So for example,
we have about a roughly 11. uh, 5
trillion dollars of uh, uh, of checkable
deposits and money market fund exposure
on the household sector balance sheet up
from about 3.5 trillion dollars prior to
the pandemic. So, we've had explosive
growth in the amount of liquidity on
household sector balance sheets. It's
about 5% of total household assets,
which is, you know, by far the highest
ratio we've seen in terms of cash
relative to total household assets. And
so, households, you know, particularly
the households that are exposed to all
that
extra wealth are very, you know, feeling
flush, you know, with cash and income
support from the federal government in a
way that is ultimately allowing them to
power through, you know, high gas
prices, power through rising food
prices, power through the deepening of
the nationwide affordability crisis that
only really truly impacts, you know, the
households on the bottom of our
increasingly K-shaped US economy. So,
don't get, you know, the consumer
confidence statistics are what they are,
and they are what they are for a reason.
Consumer confidence statistics are
terrible because every vote is counts as
one vote in a, you know, metric like
that. Whereas the aggregate economic
statistics uh, continue to be great
because there's an outsized,
increasingly outsized share of
consumption and investment from the rich
households and the rich families as and
and as well as the largest businesses in
the economy as well. So, it's, you know,
the GDP and all those types of
statistics are weighted relative to the
activity of folks at the top of the K.
And and and and statistics like consumer
confidence are unweighted in a way that
kind of exposes the true underlying
malaise that we continue to observe in
the economy. So, we'll wrap it up there.
Darius Dale here presenting our macro
minute for Tuesday, May 26th, 2026. Best
of luck out there today. We'll catch
back here tomorrow. Cheers.
